content.text: — attributes whose mere presence means true and whose absence means false. Examples include
. JSX maps these through a simple rule:

// All three are identical — the attribute is present
<input disabled />
<input disabled={true} />
<input disabled={1 === 1} />

// All three omit the attribute
<input />
<input disabled={false} />
<input disabled={null} />
<input disabled={undefined} />
. It exists because boolean attributes appear often enough that the longer form would clutter code.

does not remove the attribute — it adds it with the value "false", which the browser treats as truthy for boolean semantics.
For HTML boolean attributes, always use a JavaScript boolean expression inside

The Zero Pitfall in Conditional Children
for conditional rendering, be careful with values that are falsy but render as visible text. The number
is falsy in JavaScript, but React renders it as the character "0" rather than skipping it — unlike
The zero pitfall is among the most common React bugs. Prefer explicit comparisons or ternaries over bare
whenever the left side can be a number.
JSX uses JavaScript property names, not HTML attribute names. Two attributes differ: HTML
. Using HTML names produces a React warning and may not apply correctly.
attributes are passed through as-is because they use hyphens. React forwards them to the DOM without transformation.

Event handlers are attributes in JSX too, using camelCase names and function values — not strings.
Inline arrow functions are fine for simple cases. Inside
Attributes that expect numbers accept JS number literals directly.
setVolume(Number(e.target.value))} />
still works for most numeric HTML attributes, but number literals are clearer and avoid silent coercion surprises.

JSX attribute syntax maps cleanly onto JavaScript types: booleans control presence/absence, null/undefined/false omit attributes, and any expression in {} is valid. Key pitfalls are the string "false" trap for boolean attributes and the zero 0 pitfall in conditional children. Use className and htmlFor for the two HTML names that differ in JSX.
How JSX Attributes Map to DOM Properties
Not all JSX attributes map one-to-one to DOM attributes. React distinguishes between DOM
(the HTML you see in markup) and DOM
(JavaScript object properties on element nodes). JSX uses the property names, and React handles the mapping to DOM attributes where they differ.
React sets properties on the DOM node (
for most standard attributes, which is why you must use the property name. Custom data and ARIA attributes, which have no matching DOM property, are set via
and therefore keep their hyphenated form.
Controlled vs Uncontrolled: value and defaultValue
Form elements have two distinct attribute patterns in JSX. A
— React drives the element state. An
) — the DOM drives its own state after initial render.
Mixing the two is a common mistake. If you supply
, React warns that a controlled input cannot be changed. If you switch between
, React warns about a component switching between controlled and uncontrolled.

Pick one pattern per element and stick to it for the element's lifetime. Controlled inputs are preferred in modern React; uncontrolled are mainly useful for file inputs or integrating with non-React libraries.

Building complex class strings conditionally is common enough that the ecosystem has dedicated utilities:
are the most popular. They accept strings, objects, arrays, and falsy values, producing a trimmed space-joined class string.
is a 228-byte utility with no dependencies. It handles every conditional class pattern without template literal complexity and is widely used in component libraries.

When testing components that use computed or boolean attributes, target
attributes and query by role with attribute matchers:
checks the DOM property, not the attribute string — matching exactly what a browser or screen reader sees.
Inline object and array literals in JSX create a new reference every render. For
this is usually fine, but components wrapped in
rely on shallow comparison — new references break memo's bailout.
Extract style objects to module-level constants for static styles, or use
for style objects that depend on props or state. Stabilise handler functions with
only when the component is memoized and the handler is passed as a prop that memo checks.

TypeScript's JSX type system derives attribute types from the DOM lib and React's type definitions. For HTML elements, React exposes InputHTMLAttributes, ButtonHTMLAttributes, and similar interfaces. These include all valid attributes and their TypeScript types, so the compiler catches mismatches at build time.
attributes are typed as optional strings, even for those that accept boolean values semantically. Passing a boolean is fine — React converts it to
When rendering inline SVG in JSX, attribute names follow the same camelCase convention. SVG-specific attributes that are hyphenated in the SVG spec become camelCase in JSX:
(used in older SVG) — in modern React/SVG, use plain
React emits console warnings during development when you pass attributes it cannot handle:
Any prop that React does not recognise as a valid HTML attribute, and is not a
attribute, triggers a warning. Destructure component-only props before spreading to DOM elements.
Passing a string for a boolean attribute (
) or an object for a string attribute triggers a warning or silent bug.

React validates ARIA role values against the ARIA spec and warns on invalid values like
Always test with the development build of React — production builds strip these warnings for performance, and you will miss valuable diagnostics if you only test in prod mode.

Practice: audit a form component and replace any string-valued boolean attributes with proper JSX boolean expressions, then confirm the zero-pitfall pattern does not exist in any conditional rendering.
